mcqs.question: Given: List<String> list = Arrays.asList("Zebra", "Apple", "Mango"); Collections.sort(list, Comparator.naturalOrder()); What is the output order?
mcqs.explanation: naturalOrder() sorts in ascending alphabetical order (natural String ordering), so: Apple < Mango < Zebra

mcqs.question: What will be printed? Comparator<Integer> c = (a, b) -> b - a; List<Integer> nums = Arrays.asList(3, 1, 4, 1, 5); Collections.sort(nums, c); System.out.println(nums);
mcqs.explanation: b - a gives descending order. When b > a, result is positive (swap). So sorted descending: 5, 4, 3, 1, 1

mcqs.question: Which Comparator method is used to sort null values at the beginning of a collection?
mcqs.explanation: nullsFirst() creates a Comparator that treats null as less than all other values, placing null elements first

mcqs.question: What is the output? Comparator<String> comp = Comparator.comparingInt(String::length); String[] arr = {"Hi", "Hello", "Hey", "Good Morning"}; Arrays.sort(arr, comp); System.out.println(Arrays.toString(arr));
mcqs.explanation: comparingInt(String::length) sorts by string length ascending. Lengths: 2, 3, 5, 11 -> corresponding strings in that order

mcqs.question: What does Collections.sort(list, Comparator.reverseOrder()) do?

options.text: Sorts in descending order using natural ordering

mcqs.explanation: reverseOrder() returns a Comparator that reverses the natural ordering, so for Strings it sorts in reverse alphabetical order (descending)

mcqs.question: Given Employee class with fields name and salary. Which comparator correctly sorts by salary ascending then name ascending?
mcqs.explanation: First comparing salary (primary sort), then comparing name (secondary sort for equal salaries). Both use natural (ascending) order by default

mcqs.question: What is the result of comparing("Banana", "Apple") using naturalOrder()?
mcqs.explanation: naturalOrder compares lexicographically. 'Banana' > 'Apple' lexicographically (B > A), so returns positive value

mcqs.question: Which statement is true about comparing(a, b) returning 0?

options.text: a and b are equal in all respects
a and b are considered equal for ordering purposes (equals() may differ)
a and b must be the same object

mcqs.explanation: When compare() returns 0, elements are considered equal for sorting. They may not be equal via equals(), but ordering-wise they're equivalent

mcqs.question: What will happen if compare() violates transitivity (a > b, b > c, but a < c)?
mcqs.explanation: Violating transitivity doesn't cause immediate error but leads to unpredictable sort behavior, often appearing to work but producing wrong results

mcqs.question: Given a list with nulls: Arrays.asList(null, "B", null, "A"). Which sort produces [null, null, A, B]?
mcqs.explanation: nullsLast() puts nulls at end, naturalOrder() sorts non-nulls alphabetically, giving nulls first then A, B

mcqs.question: Which is the correct way to create a case-insensitive String comparator?
mcqs.explanation: Comparator.comparing(keyExtractor) uses natural order on extracted key. toLowerCase() returns String sorted case-insensitively (a == A)

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of using Collections.sort() with Comparator?
mcqs.explanation: Collections.sort() uses merge sort (Java 7+) which is O(n log n) time complexity. Comparator.compare() is called O(n log n) times

mcqs.question: What does thenComparingInt() optimize compared to thenComparing()?

options.text: Avoids autoboxing overhead for primitive comparisons

mcqs.explanation: thenComparingInt() uses primitive int, avoiding Integer autoboxing/unboxing overhead when comparing int values

mcqs.question: Which Comparator static factory method returns a Comparator for an object's natural order?
mcqs.explanation: naturalOrder() returns a Comparator that imposes the natural ordering of the type (uses Comparable interface)

mcqs.question: Given: Stream.of(3,1,4,1,5).sorted(Comparator.reverseOrder()).forEach(System.out::print); What is output?
mcqs.explanation: sorted(Comparator.reverseOrder()) sorts stream elements in descending order. Sorted: 5, 4, 3, 1, 1
